title: List Comprehensions
description: Create lists in a Pythonic way.

slides.title: Pythonic List Creation 🐍
slides.content:
# List Comprehensions

Compact way to create lists:

```python
# Traditional
squares = []
for x in range(5):
    squares.append(x**2)

# Comprehension
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]
```

Same result, one line!

slides.title: Basic Comprehension 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
# Traditional loop
traditional = []
for i in range(5):
    traditional.append(i * 2)
print("Traditional:", traditional)

# List comprehension
comprehension = [i * 2 for i in range(5)]
print("Comprehension:", comprehension)

# They're the same!
print("Equal:", traditional == comprehension)

quizData.question: What is [x*2 for x in range(3)]?

slides.title: Syntax Breakdown 📝
slides.content:
# Comprehension Anatomy

```python
[expression for item in iterable if condition]
```

* **expression** — What to put in list
* **item** — Current element
* **iterable** — What to loop over
* **condition** — Optional filter

slides.title: With Conditions 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
# Only even numbers
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print("Evens:", evens)

# Only positive from list
nums = [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3]
positives = [n for n in nums if n > 0]
print("Positives:", positives)

# Filter strings
words = ["hello", "hi", "hey", "goodbye"]
short = [w for w in words if len(w) <= 3]
print("Short words:", short)

quizData.question: [x for x in range(5) if x > 2] gives?

slides.title: With Expressions ✨
slides.content:
# Transform Elements

```python
# Uppercase
[s.upper() for s in ["a", "b", "c"]]
# ['A', 'B', 'C']

# Conditional expression
["even" if x%2==0 else "odd" for x in range(4)]
# ['even', 'odd', 'even', 'odd']
```

slides.title: Complex Expressions 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
names = ["alice", "bob", "charlie"]

# Transform
upper_names = [name.upper() for name in names]
print("Upper:", upper_names)

# Conditional expression
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
labeled = [f"{n} is even" if n % 2 == 0 else f"{n} is odd" for n in numbers]
for label in labeled:
    print(label)

slides.title: Dict & Set Comprehensions 📦
Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## When to Use Comprehensions

✅ **Use when:**
* Creating a new list from existing data
* Simple transformations
* Filtering

❌ **Don't use when:**
* Complex logic (> 1 line)
* Side effects needed
* Readability suffers

quizData.question: What makes comprehensions 'Pythonic'?
quizData.explanation: Comprehensions are considered Pythonic because they're concise and elegant!
